title: Paper Explained: Convergent Evolution: How Different Language Models Learn Similar Number Representations - A Beginner's Guide
subtitle: How Different AI Models Learn Similar Numbers

content.background:
Before this work, researchers had a puzzling picture about how language models think about numbers. Models trained on regular text sometimes show funny, rhythmic patterns in how they represent numbers—like certain “frequencies” or periodic signals with spikes every 2, 5, or 10 steps. Yet these same patterns show up in many different model families (Transformers, RNNs, embeddings), and in some cases the models could linearly separate numbers by their remainder when divided by T, while in others they could not. In short, we could observe a common numerical signature across models, but we didn’t understand why some models could actually reason about numbers (modulo arithmetic) in a simple, human-like way and others couldn’t. That ambiguity left a gap between what the math looked like inside the model and what the model could actually do with numbers.

This gap matters because if we want AI systems that reason about numbers reliably, we need to know what kinds of representations naturally emerge and under what conditions they become truly useful for arithmetic-like tasks. Without this knowledge, we risk engineering efforts that work only for a specific architecture or training setup, or missing deeper limits of what models can learn from language data. The paper asks practical questions: What signals in the training data (such as how numbers appear next to other words or with other numbers) and what choices in architecture, optimization, or tokenization drive models toward representations that can be linearly read as mod-T answers? By exploring these questions, the work aims to explain why many different models end up with similar number-related features and to illuminate the pathways through which useful numeric reasoning can arise.
content.methodology:
Think of what a language model learns about numbers as a kind of hidden rhythm or melody inside its internal representations. The researchers found that, when trains on ordinary text, these models pick up repeating patterns with certain “beats” (periods) of 2, 5, and 10. In other words, there are features in the model’s internal signals that tend to repeat every 2 words, every 5 words, or every 10 words. They also discovered a two-layer structure to these patterns: in the Fourier (frequency) view, many models show those spikes, but only some models develop a geometry that makes it easy to tell numbers apart by their remainder when divided by a period T (mod-T) using a simple, straight-line decision. So you can have strong rhythmic signals without easy linear separability of mod-T.

What the researchers did, in simple steps:
- They trained a variety of models on natural language data: Transformers, Linear RNNs, LSTMs, and even classic word embeddings trained in different ways.
- They analyzed the internal representations to detect those period-2, period-5, and period-10 features in the frequency domain.
- They then asked a straightforward test: can you draw a straight line that separates numbers by their remainder mod-T using those features? Some models pass this test (geometrically separable), others don’t, even though they all show the same kind of rhythmic signals in the frequency view.
- They also proved, at a conceptual level, that having sparse (peaky) Fourier content is necessary for any hope of geometric separability, but it isn’t enough by itself to guarantee it.

Empirically, they explored what helps a model develop those usable, linearly separable features. They found two routes:
- Route A: The model learns from general language signals that co-occur with numbers, such as numbers appearing near certain words or interacting across numbers. These co-occurrence cues shape the geometry of the representations so that mod-T becomes linearly separable.
- Route B: The model learns from explicit multi-token arithmetic cues—things in the data that require adding numbers across several tokens. This arithmetic signal pushes the representations toward the kind of geometric separability that supports simple mod-T classification.
Importantly, these two routes show that different training signals can lead to the same end result: a convergent set of features across very different model families. The broader takeaway is that diverse models can end up learning similar numeric representations, just guided by different training signals—what the authors call convergent evolution in feature learning.

Overall, the work highlights that models can converge on similar number representations even when their architectures differ, and that the path to those representations depends on the data, the learning setup, and even the tokenizer. It sheds light on why neural models may develop useful numeric reasoning features, and it points to concrete ways training data and tasks influence whether those features are simply present in the frequency spectrum or actually usable for simple, linear reasoning about numbers modulo T.
content.results:
This work investigates how different language models end up representing numbers when they learn from natural language. The key finding is that many models—ranging from Transformers to recurrent nets and even word embeddings—develop similar periodic patterns in their internal representations with dominant cycles at short periods like 2, 5, and 10. Beyond this shared spectral signal, the authors distinguish a second, deeper layer: only some models form geometric, linearly separable features that let you classify numbers by their remainder modulo T. In short, there’s a two-level story—a common rhythmic signature across models, and, for some models, a separate, easy-to-use numeric grouping emerges.

The paper provides both a theoretical and empirical view of why this happens. It shows that having sparse Fourier-style features (the spectral spikes) is a necessary condition for being able to separate numbers by modulo T with a simple linear boundary, but it is not sufficient by itself. That means you can see the right rhythms in a model’s representations without actually having a straightforward way to read off a number’s mod-T class. Through experiments, the authors show that whether a model attains these geometrically separable features depends on a mix of factors: the training data, the architecture, the optimization method, and even how numbers are tokenized. They identify two practical routes for acquiring separable number representations: (1) learning from broad language signals where numbers co-occur and influence each other, and (2) learning from multi-token addition-style tasks that explicitly require combining several tokens to compute a result.

In terms of impact, this work helps explain why different model families can end up “converging” on similar numeric representations even when their architectures and training signals differ. It provides a conceptual bridge between spectral properties (the periodic signals seen in the model’s internal representations) and a usable numeric ability (modulo arithmetic via a linear readout). Practically, it suggests new ways to steer numeric reasoning in language models: choose data, architectures, optimizers, and tokenization schemes that encourage not just the right rhythms but also the right geometric separability, depending on the task. The findings also offer a clearer lens for interpreting why models sometimes fail at numeric tasks and how to design training setups that promote robust number understanding, without needing hand-crafted modules.
content.significance:
This paper matters today because numbers are a common pain point for large language models (LLMs). Even when models are trained on vast natural text, they still show only an approximate sense of arithmetic and numeric relations. The authors reveal that many different model families (Transformers, RNNs, LSTMs, even classic word embeddings) end up with similar periodic patterns in how they represent numbers, but only some of these patterns are easy to use for exact counting or modular arithmetic with a simple, linearly separable rule. In other words, the same training signals can produce convergent numerical representations across diverse architectures, but whether those representations can be read out by simple tools depends on whether they are geometrically separable. This helps explain both why LLMs can be surprisingly good at some number tasks and why they still stumble at others, and it highlights the delicate interplay between data, model design, and training objectives when it comes to numbers.

The paper also influenced later work by clarifying where numeracy comes from in these models. It shows two practical routes to good numeric reasoning: (1) exposure to co-occurrence signals in natural language, including how numbers appear next to other numbers or in numeric interactions, and (2) explicit multi-token arithmetic training, such as teaching the model to perform addition across several tokens. This has guided subsequent research and tooling: designers began paying closer attention to tokenizer choices, data mixes, and targeted arithmetic tasks when building or fine-tuning models. It also fed into a broader trend of incorporating numerical reasoning checks or external calculators into AI systems, and it helped researchers understand when a simple linear readout can extract a numeric property versus when more complex, perhaps multi-step reasoning is needed. Overall, it pushed the field toward a more nuanced view of how numbers are grounded in language-only training versus when they require additional structure or tools.

In terms of real-world systems people use today, the ideas from this work underpin how modern AI assistants like ChatGPT and other large-language systems handle numbers in practice. The recognition that numeric representations can be learned in convergent ways but may or may not be linearly readable supports the common engineering choice to pair LLMs with external calculators or function-calling capabilities for precise arithmetic. It also informs the ongoing push to design better numeric benchmarks, safer math tutoring tools, and finance or coding assistants that can rely on explicit numeric modules when needed. The lasting significance is that it gives researchers and practitioners a clearer map of when internal representations are enough and when we should lean on external, structured arithmetic aids—an idea central to building trustworthy, capable AI that can reason with numbers as reliably as humans do.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding Fourier Domain Sparsity: The Heart of Convergent Evolution
concept_explanation.content:
Think of numbers in text like notes in a song, and the model’s internal representations of those numbers as the melody. Fourier Domain Sparsity is basically about the melody having only a few very strong rhythms. When a model learns to represent numbers, the hidden signals can show up as repeating patterns with specific "periods" (like a beat every 2 tokens, every 5 tokens, or every 10 tokens). If most of the information about a number sits in just a handful of these rhythm-like components, we say the Fourier spectrum is sparse. In the paper, they find dominant periods at T = 2, 5, and 10. This is like noticing that the song mainly uses three clear rhythms instead of a lot of complicated, undefined background noise.

Here’s how it works step by step, in simple terms. First, the model processes a sentence containing numbers and builds internal vectors to represent each number. Second, if you look at how those vectors change across many numbers, you can imagine breaking that change into a sum of sine and cosine waves—this is the Fourier view. In many models, a few strong waves line up at periods 2, 5, and 10, which is what the paper calls Fourier domain sparsity. Third, because these waves are aligned with certain regular spacings, you can imagine, in principle, separating numbers by their remainder when divided by T (modulo T) with a simple linear decision boundary, using those wave-based features. In other words, the presence of strong, simple rhythms makes a linear line (or plane) in the feature space potentially capable of grouping numbers by mod-T.

The paper emphasizes an important technical point: having these sparse Fourier features is necessary but not sufficient for easy linear separability by mod-T. “Necessary” means you would likely not get a clean mod-T division without some strong periodic signals in the first place. “Not sufficient” means that even with clear spikes at T = 2, 5, 10, the geometry of those features might still be arranged in a way that a straight line can’t separate the classes. You can picture it like having a few dominant rhythms, but if those rhythms line up in a way that doesn’t produce distinct “clusters” for each remainder, a simple linear boundary won’t do the job. So you need not only the right rhythms, but also the right alignment and phase relationships among features so that numbers with the same mod-T remainder land on one side of a straight line and other remainders land on the other side.

The authors find two main routes by which models can acquire these geometrically separable features. One route is learning from complementary co-occurrence signals in natural language—numbers often appear with other numbers or with certain words, and cross-number interactions can teach the model to align its features into a partition that reflects mod-T. The other route comes from explicit arithmetic tasks, like multi-token addition problems, where the model must combine several numbers to produce a result; this pushes the internal features toward a geometry that cleanly separates residues mod-T. The paper also notes that the data, the model architecture (Transformers, various RNN variants, etc.), the optimizer, and even the tokenizer all influence whether and how these separable features emerge. In short, many different training signals can lead to the same kind of number-representing features, a phenomenon they call convergent evolution.

This idea has practical implications. If you care about models that reason about numbers or perform arithmetic, you can design data and tasks to encourage the right kind of Fourier-sparse, geometrically separable features—e.g., include systematic number co-occurrence patterns or explicitly multi-token arithmetic problems. You can also use Fourier-domain analysis as a diagnostic tool: by inspecting the spectral content of a model’s hidden representations for numbers, you can gauge whether the model is developing the kind of rhythmic structure that supports modular reasoning, and you can test how changes to data, architecture, or training affect that structure. Ultimately, understanding Fourier domain sparsity helps explain why different models—though trained on different data and with different recipes—end up learning surprisingly similar numeric representations, and it guides us in building models that are better at handling numbers, math, and modular reasoning in text.

summary: This paper shows that diverse language models converge on similar number representations by learning periodic Fourier features, reveals that only some of these features become linearly separable for mod-T tasks (with Fourier sparsity being necessary but not sufficient), and demonstrates that data, architecture, optimizer, and tokenizer steer models toward two routes—co-occurrence signals in language data or multi-token addition—revealing convergent evolution of arithmetic-like representations.
